diagrams.title: Demand Paging Memory Architecture
diagrams.description: Shows the three-tier memory hierarchy: virtual address space (disk/swap), physical RAM, and CPU. Illustrates how pages reside in swap space and are loaded into physical memory only when accessed (demand paging). Visualizes the page tables tracking which pages are in memory vs. on disk.
diagrams.purpose: Explains the fundamental concept of demand paging - that pages are brought from disk to memory only when referenced, not all at program load time

diagrams.title: Page Fault Handling Flow
diagrams.description: A flowchart diagram showing the step-by-step process of handling a page fault: CPU generates memory reference → Check page table → Page not in memory (invalid) → OS takes control → Locate page on disk → Find free frame OR select victim page → Read page from disk to frame → Update page table → Restart the instruction
diagrams.purpose: Explains the complete page fault handling sequence and the role of the operating system in bringing pages into memory

diagrams.title: Page Replacement: FIFO Example
diagrams.description: Demonstrates the First-In-First-Out (FIFO) page replacement algorithm in action. Shows a physical memory with limited frames, a reference string, and how pages are loaded and evicted over time. Visualizes the 'anomaly' where adding more frames can increase page faults.
diagrams.purpose: Illustrates how page replacement works when memory is full and a new page must be brought in - demonstrates the FIFO algorithm with its advantages and anomalies
